result:
On the nights when fog sits low, the radio fills with a kind of animal breathing. Static grows fur along every syllable. You can lean toward it, cup your hands like you might around a candle, and feel the heat of other lives carry through. I keep a little logbook near the transmitter, the lined kind with feint blue veins and a place for time, wind, and remarks. Remarks are where the truth lives.

It’s an old lighthouse. The stairs complain in a voice that’s part rust, part song. The prisms of the lens wear a sleeve of salt, and though I clean them twice a day there’s always a lace of the ocean left clinging. The light turns, unhurried, a steady arc laid over water like the arm of someone promising to stay. Sometimes the promise is enough. Sometimes it isn’t.

The fox comes on Wednesdays. I don’t know how it tells time—maybe by the routines that stack themselves up here like bowls—but there it is at the door when the kitchen smells of onions. It looks at me with the polite exasperation of a neighbor who knows I am too soft to say no. I tear a piece of bread and the fox takes it gently, as if I am a fawn-hearted thing that could shatter. On the weeks when the fog refuses to lift, that small transaction feels like proof that language is not all we have.

I learned the names of stars from my sister on the roof of the house where we didn’t live for long. She would tip her head back until her hair made a river and say, there’s Deneb, see? Vega’s the bright one, Altair’s over there; they make a triangle but also a bird. It was important to her that I notice how the same points could be called different things depending on the map. We put plates over our hearts like armor and held sticks like oars and promised that someday we’d find a sea of light. She kept her promises better than I kept mine.

The logbook I use belonged to two keepers before me. Their handwriting leans different directions. One used to underline wind: fresh breeze, then stiff breeze underlined twice. Another drew tiny clouds with little stitches of rain falling from their bellies. Between entries they left small surprises: a pressed leaf, a faded receipt for kerosene, one perfect cartoon of a gull wearing boots. I add my own offerings—stripes of radio chatter, a red ring around the night we lost power, a note about the whale that slept under a skin of moonlight and sounded like a door closing gently.

What comes through on the radio is a census of the immediate world. Fishermen trading recipes with the same exactness they use for coordinates. A woman who radios every year on her father’s birthday to say, I remember, I remember, I remember, and then there’s only weather. Once a pilot whose voice shook like glass until I said, say what you can see, and he did—white shingles, a windbreak of pines, the hillside like a shoulder—and in the saying found his way.

I keep a box of index cards on the shelf with the cups. Each card carries a small thing I want to return to: the light of a ferry swallowing a whole school of moths, phosphorescence on a broken wave like teeth in the dark, thirteen pelicans low as punctuation. On nights when the sea sounds like it’s chewing its own name I shuffle these cards and try to remember that I have been alive in ways that did not ache.

Some days the mainland feels like a rumor I half believe. They say most ships don’t need lights anymore, not really. There are satellites and systems and digital charts that update while you are blinking. But I have watched a captain make a long slow turn toward a beam of old light because every other screen had gone strange with rain or with error, and that beam said, I am here, the world is still particular, your hull still holds.

The storm that made a myth of itself began as a warm edge to the wind. I wrote the numbers: barometer falling, waves building, remarks: gulls absurdly cheerful, fox absent. When it hit, it hit all at once, the way people leave when they’re done promising they won’t. The generator coughed awake and I went room to room like an usher, checking the seals, the lantern fuel, the little graveyard of fuses we keep for luck. The tower flexed like a rib cage. That I am always astonished by how everything moves is, I think, how I stay.

Around midnight a voice smudged itself across the band. It was young and trying very hard not to be. We took a wave wrong, the voice said. The jib’s a mess. The engine—okay, the engine is not okay. I asked for position and got a string of numbers with a wobble in it, like a thread pulled through thick cloth. Say what you can see, I said, and there was a long sleeve of rain between us, but then: a little island like two fists, some white water, a blinking light that could be a boat or could be a trick. I told them the rock’s name and didn’t tell them that the name means the place where you learn how sharp something is.

I spoke until my throat felt like old rope. I said hold what you can, I said keep the bow to it, I said you don’t have to be brave, just busy. The light made its steady turn. Once a flash caught the rain at the right angle and the whole world was needles. In the log I wrote: voices cut out, then back in; hummingbird heart in my hand. I don’t know why I wrote that except I do.

They came around the fist of rock like people changing their minds. The hull juddered; the radio filled with a sound like applause, unearned but welcome. Later, a small voice in the bleed of static said thank you, and I wrote that too. Remarks: gratitude, unplaceable. Wind: ridiculous.

Morning is a different country from night and I am a poor citizen of both. The storm left a grammar on the beach: commas of driftwood, a question mark made of kelp, a whole paragraph of bladderwrack shrilling under the touch of sun. The fox returned and we made our small treaty again. I boiled coffee so black it could extinguish itself. The light rested. It always rests as if it never had to work, and I forgive it because I know better.

In the town they have a market that smells like peaches even in months when peaches are a kind of fiction. The clerk tells me news as if I have been living underwater, which in a way I have. There’s always someone building something, someone leaving, someone making a fuss about the big clock that refuses to keep good time. I buy more onions than I need and a cheap deck of cards because I like the promise that something can be shuffled until it is fair.

Sometimes, when the frequency is clear, I talk low into the radio to no one in particular. I say the names of things: reef, shoal, bay, drift, scud, wrack, spindrift, fetch. I say lighthouse like a person would say home and mean it in the old sense, as in where you place your tools when you are finished and the tools remember you. I like to think the words go out and stick to the air, and when someone needs them they find their hands landing in the right place.

It would be cleaner storytelling if I said that every night has a climax, that every trouble finds land. The truth is softer and more bearable: most nights nothing much happens and I write it down anyway. Wind: tired. Sea: agreeable. Remarks: fox certain, stars late. There’s a tenderness in accounting for the unremarkable. It’s the kind of tenderness that, once you learn it, you start seeing everywhere.

If anyone asks what the job is, I say it’s keeping. Not rescuing, not warning, not shining—though those happen too—but keeping: a light, a list, a promise, a habit, a name. The map I carry is not the kind you fold but the kind that lives behind the eyes, that bright braid of where the cries came from and where they went. When I’m old, if I am lucky enough to be old, I will sit somewhere with a soft chair and a window and shuffle my cards and say, once, in the fog, someone said I remember three times like a bell. And if no one is there to hear it, the air will keep it. The air keeps almost everything, if you ask it gently.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
